1: 'At night on my bed I searched for the one I love' — the night search is a literary device common in ANE love poetry; whether this is a literal experience or a dream-vision is debated. Egyptian love poetry uses the same motif: the lover wandering in search of the beloved. The repetition 'I searched for him but did not find him' creates the tension of longing and absence that drives the poem. The bed (*mishkav*) is the natural place of rest and intimacy; the beloved's absence from it is what prompts the search.
2: 'I will go about the city, through the streets and squares' — the city (*ir*) as the setting of danger and search; the open squares (*rechovot*) and narrow streets (*shevaqim*) of ancient Jerusalem or an idealized city. A woman alone in the city at night was socially transgressive in ancient near eastern society: women's public movement was restricted by honor-shame conventions that linked female exposure to family honor. The beloved's willingness to violate these conventions for love's sake marks the intensity of her longing.
3: 'The city guards found me as they made their rounds' — the *shomerim* (watchmen/guards) were the night security of ancient cities, patrolling the streets to prevent crime and enforce order. Their encounter with the woman alone at night is neutral here (they are asked a question and implicitly answer by her movement); contrast the hostile encounter in Song of Solomon 5:7. The question 'have you seen the one I love?' — asking strangers for information about a private beloved — makes her vulnerability public, a transgression of the propriety that would normally keep such a search private.
4: 'I had barely passed them when I found the one I love — I held him and would not let him go' — the language *achaztihu* ('I held him/seized him') echoes Genesis 32:26 where Jacob refuses to release the wrestling figure: 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.' The tenacity of the beloved's grip on her lover is cast in the language of the most famous wrestling encounter in the OT — love's grip as persistent as faith's grip. The 'mother's house' (*beit immi*) rather than 'father's house' is unusual; it may reflect the woman's domestic sphere or a matrilocal marriage custom.
5: The refrain repeated (cf. Song of Solomon 2:7): 'I charge you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases.' The context here follows the successful finding and holding; the charge is a kind of warning after the danger of the search — love has been found but its awakening and timing remain precious and not to be disturbed by others. The repetition of the charge after the dream-episode reinforces its force.
6: 'Who is this coming up from the desert like a column of smoke?' — the image of a column of smoke (*timrot ashan*) from the wilderness evokes the divine presence in the Exodus: the pillar of cloud by day and fire by night (Exodus 13:21-22; Numbers 14:14). A royal procession emerging from the desert trailing fragrance — myrrh (*mor*), frankincense (*levonah*), and merchants' spices — would have been a dramatic sight. The question 'who is this?' is the same form as the question about Wisdom in Proverbs 31:10 and about the eschatological figures in Isaiah 63:1.
7: 'The royal litter of Solomon, surrounded by sixty warriors' — the *mittato* (litter/couch of Solomon) served as the conveyance for a royal procession or bridal transport. The sixty *gibbore hagibborum* (mighty men of the mighty) echo David's mighty men — the thirty (*shaloshim*) and other elite warriors of 2 Sam 23. Here sixty — twice thirty — forms a royal bodyguard; they are 'skilled in war, each with sword at his side, ready for danger in the night.'
8: 'All of them carry swords, skilled in battle, each with sword at side, ready for danger in the night' — the *pachad ballailot* (dread of the night) was a real security concern for ancient processions; bandit attacks on traveling parties were a constant threat in the ancient near east. The armed escort of the royal litter represents the intersection of love and power: the most intimate transport is guarded by the most capable soldiers. The *gibbor* (mighty man) as escort figure appears in royal and military contexts throughout the OT.
9: 'King Solomon made his royal litter from Lebanese cedar' — the *apiryon* (bridal carriage or palanquin; a loanword possibly from Sanskrit *paryanka* via Persian) was a covered carrying-chair used for royal and bridal transport. Lebanese cedar (*erez ha'levanon*) was the finest available building material (1 Kgs 5-7; Psalms 92:12); its use for the litter indicates the highest luxury. The choice of cedar connects the beloved's bridal transport to the materials of the temple — love housed in temple-grade material.
10: 'Its posts are silver, its back of gold, its seat purple, its interior lovingly lined by the daughters of Jerusalem' — the materials echo the tabernacle and temple furnishings: silver (*kesef*) posts, gold (*zahav*) back, purple (*argaman*) seat — all are exact descriptions of tabernacle materials (Exod 26-27). The interior 'lovingly lined' (*ratsuf ahavah*) by the daughters of Jerusalem makes the bridal litter a collaborative act of the community's love. Purple dye was extracted from shellfish (*Murex*) at Tyre and Sidon and was the most expensive fabric color in the ancient world.
11: 'Come out, daughters of Zion, and gaze on King Solomon with the crown his mother crowned him on his wedding day' — the *atarah* (crown) used here is the wedding crown, distinct from the royal crown (*nezer*). Jewish tradition associated the wedding crown with joy; the Mishnah notes that wedding crowns were banned after the destruction of the temple as a sign of mourning. The phrase 'the day his heart rejoiced' (*simchat libo*) uses the heart (*lev*) for the seat of joy; the wedding day is the day of the heart's fullness — a statement about love's consummation as the highest human joy.
